label: verify-prop-218
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- Prop 218

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …ed the debt-service allocation as squarely traceable to the cost rationale and likely to survive, supporting a "substantial part" rather than "whole" outcome.

**Theme I — Article X, §2 / Water Code §375 cannot override Prop 218's specific text (2 of 7, both at 70).**
Two panelists expressly reasoned that the conservation-mandate defense fails against the later-adopted, more specific §6(b) requirements.

**Cross-cutting uncertainty (flagged by …

[tetlock] …he funds required to provide the property related service,' or §6(b)(3)'s proportionality requirement — i.e., is cross-subsidization from high-tier payers to non-payer rebate recipients categorically impermissible under Prop 218? | 55 | Even if the $0.85 rate could be justified purely by cost-of-service, redirecting most of the money to benefit different customers (rather than the class actually causing the marginal cost) is a distinct and pote…

[dialectic] …rd of Review: No Deference to the District's Rate-Setting Judgment

This matters enormously because water agencies are accustomed to rational-basis deference on rate design (as in ordinary municipal utility ratemaking). Prop 218 displaces that deference for the specific cost-of-service and proportionality inquiries:

- Courts apply **independent judicial review** to whether a fee complies with § 6(b)'s substantive requirements — the agency's ow…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Proposition 218 (Nov. 1996), codified at Cal. Const. art. XIII C & art. XIII D; substantive fee requirements at art. XIII D, § 6(b) (incl. § 6(b)(1) cost-of-service cap and § 6(b)(3) proportionality requirement)","note":"Prop 218 exists and is correctly identified with Article XIII D, §6(b)'s cost-of-service and parcel-proportionality requirements (§6(b)(1), (b)(3)); the snippets' description of a cross-subsidization concern under §6(b)(3) and of courts applying independent-judgment/no-deference review to rate decisions is consistent with the constitutional text and with case law such as Silicon Valley Taxpayers Ass'n v. Santa Clara County Open Space Authority (2008) 44 Cal.4th 431. The passing reference to Article X, §2/Water Code §375 is used as a separate, competing conservation-doctrine authority, not as an alternate label for Prop 218, so it doesn't affect this cite's accuracy.","source_urls":["https://law.justia.com/constitution/california/article-xiii-d/section-6/","https://lao.ca.gov/1996/120196_prop_218/understanding_prop218_1296.html","https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/1996_California_Proposition_218","https://www.downeybrand.com/legal-alerts/in-a-landmark-case-interpreting-proposition-218-the-california-supreme-court-expands-the-judiciarys-role-in-reviewing-the-adequacy-of-local-benefit-assessments/"]}

data.canonical_cite: Proposition 218 (Nov. 1996), codified at Cal. Const. art. XIII C & art. XIII D; substantive fee requirements at art. XIII D, § 6(b) (incl. § 6(b)(1) cost-of-service cap and § 6(b)(3) proportionality requirement)
data.note: Prop 218 exists and is correctly identified with Article XIII D, §6(b)'s cost-of-service and parcel-proportionality requirements (§6(b)(1), (b)(3)); the snippets' description of a cross-subsidization concern under §6(b)(3) and of courts applying independent-judgment/no-deference review to rate decisions is consistent with the constitutional text and with case law such as Silicon Valley Taxpayers Ass'n v. Santa Clara County Open Space Authority (2008) 44 Cal.4th 431. The passing reference to Article X, §2/Water Code §375 is used as a separate, competing conservation-doctrine authority, not as an alternate label for Prop 218, so it doesn't affect this cite's accuracy.
